FT_IC1.evidence: Paper describes the U.S. ABCD Study with U.S. recruitment sites and baseline ages 9-10 years

FT_IC5.evidence: Review/perspective paper outlining potential future technologies and methods for ABCD data collection; no original empirical analysis of ABCD data reported

notes: Non-empirical paper discussing future incorporation of mobile/wearable/social media technologies into ABCD; no original data analysis

FT_IC1.evidence: Cohort is the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) study, Netherlands (n=2354). This is the Dutch ABCD, not the U.S. ABCD Study. Methods state children were 5-6 years old from the Netherlands.

notes: Dutch ABCD cohort; radiofrequency-EMF exposure (RF-EMF) study with cell phone and cordless phone use measured; cognitive outcomes (speed of information processing, inhibitory control, cognitive flexibility, visuomotor coordination) are individual-trait but cohort is not U.S. ABCD

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=9475, baseline mean age 9.9 years; uses ABCD 5.0 data release; multisite longitudinal study

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression examining associations between sociodemographic predictors and first smartphone ownership age

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Pediatrics, published February 2026

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone ownership (age of first smartphone, binary outcome <10 years vs. ≥10 years) qualifies as a smartphone variable on Step 1. However, all predictors are demographic-only: sex, race/ethnicity, household income, household structure, parental education. The criteria binding clarification states: "If the only analyses pair the SM/phone variable with demographic-only or contextual-only on the other side, FT-IC2 = NOT_MET regardless of whether the SM/phone variable itself qualifies on Step 1 or Step 2."

notes: Paper examines sociodemographic correlates of early smartphone ownership using logistic regression with odds ratios reported. The individual-trait-level requirement is not met because only demographic variables appear on the other side of the relationship.
